attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man from a nursing home is admitted to the short-stay geriatrics unit with a two-day history of worsening agitation and confusion. His medical history includes Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson disease, atrial fibrillation, hypertension and osteoarthritis. For the previous year, he has been taking oxazepam 7.5mg BD and memantine 20mg mane. Both of these medications have provided some benefit in lessening his intrusive wandering and aggressive behaviour in the nursing home.

Over the last few hours, he has been very unsettled on the ward, making verbal and physical threats against the nursing staff. He has has been wandering into the rooms of fellow patients and making them frightened. The geriatrics consultant would like to prescribe a PRN medication to help settle these behaviours and enable an organic delirium screen to be performed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following medications is most appropriate in this scenario?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is admitted to the geriatrics ward with an acute worsening of confusion and agitation on a background of behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) and Parkinson disease. In cases like this one, it is important to exclude an underlying organic cause. In order to allow a thorough investigative work-up to occur, additional medications for agitation may need to be prescribed, particularly if the patient is proving resistant to care.

The correct answer to this question is oral quetiapine. Belonging to the second-generation antipsychotic drug class, which comprise medications that are less likely to cause extra-pyramidal side effects than first-generation antipsychotic medications. Quetiapine is the safest second-generation antipsychotic in patients with Parkinson disease. Furthermore, this medication is available via the oral route, which is preferable to intramuscular if the patient is willing to accept medications voluntarily.

Intramuscular droperidol should only be administered in controlled environments where ongoing patient monitoring and airway support is available. As such, the ward is generally not an appropriate setting in which to give this medication, unless there are extenuating circumstances. In addition, given that droperidol is a first-generation antipsychotic and associated with extra-pyramidal side effects, it is ill-advised to give it to a patient with Parkinson disease.

Oral olanzapine is a more effective medication choice for agitation than oral quetiapine, however is associated with a greater risk of extra-pyramidal side effects. As this patient already has a pre-existing history of Parkinson disease, olanzapine is best avoided. As such, this answer option is incorrect.

Zuclopenthixol ('Clopixol Acuphase') is a first-generation antipsychotic agent. The decision to use this medication should be made by psychiatrists and only as a last resort when other pharmacological options have failed. The duration of effect is prolonged compared to the other options, making this medication an unsuitable choice. Furthermore, an intramuscular route is less preferable than the oral route in the first instance.

Intramuscular midazolam can be useful when rapidly trying to de-escalate hostile and aggressive behaviour. In this instance however, there is no reason to give intramuscular medications until oral medications have been offered. As per many hospital protocols, intramuscular clonazpepam is thought to be a safer choice than intramuscular midazolam if the oral route is not an option. Clonazepam offers a longer and more stable duration of effect, whereas midazolam is more short-acting. Midazolam is also associated with a a greater risk of respiratory depression.
learningPoints: Quetiapine is the safest second-generation antipsychotic in patients with Parkinson disease.
references: Vivancos F, Ybott I. Quetiapine for behavioral disorders management in Parkinson disease. J Neurol Sci. 2015;357:434. Available at: https://www.jns-journal.com/article/S0022-510X(15)02148-6/fulltext
